statement.markdown: Given an integer `n`, return the number of distinct configurations for placing `n` queens on an `n x n` chessboard such that no row or column contains more than one queen. The chessboard can be rotated to an equivalent configuration, and the same applies to the queens. Write a function `countDistinctConfigurations` that returns the number of distinct configurations. This function takes a list of integers representing the positions of queens, but it has a bug that needs to be fixed.
statement.inputFormat: Input format: list of integers representing positions of queens, where each queen is represented by a pair of integers (i, j) indicating its position at row `i` and column `j` on the board, and `n` as an integer representing the size of the board.
statement.outputFormat: Output format: an integer representing the number of distinct configurations.
statement.notes: You can use the `backtracking` approach to solve this problem. Note that the `backtracking` function has a bug and needs to be fixed.

examples.explanation: There are two possible configurations for the given positions of queens: [[0, 0], [2, 2], [1, 1]] and [[0, 0], [1, 1], [2, 2]].

hints.text: Use a set to store unique configurations.

editorial.title: N-Queens II Editorial
editorial.intuition: The key idea is to use a set to store unique configurations. We can represent each configuration as a string of characters where a `1` at position `i` and `j` indicates that there is a queen at row `i` and column `j`.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach would involve generating all possible configurations and checking if they are valid.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a recursive backtracking approach to generate configurations.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can use a set to store unique configurations and a recursive backtracking approach to generate configurations.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness can be shown using induction and showing that each configuration added to the set is unique.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run can involve generating a few configurations and checking if they are added to the set correctly.
commonMistakes: Not using a set to store unique configurations
